Title: Genetic enhancement of oil content in potato tuber (Solanum tuberosum L.) through an integrated metabolic engineering strategy

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy-dense storage oil; relevant for food and industrial uses
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Lipid droplets in tuber and leaf cells
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Lipid droplet surface / endoplasmic reticulum-associated lipid body monolayer
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highest among the three transgenes in most transgenic tubers and also expressed in leaves due to promoter leakiness
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse growth; developing tubers and leaves examined
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances TAG accumulation/retention by promoting lipid droplet packaging and protection in potato when coexpressed with WRI1 and DGAT1
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transgenic overexpression in gene stack; qRT-PCR; Nile Red staining; TEM visualization of lipid droplets; lipid profiling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. siOLEOSIN served as the 'Protect' component in the engineering strategy. 2. High siOLEOSIN expression was associated with high TAG accumulation in transgenic potato. 3. Transgenic tubers accumulated abundant lipid droplets, consistent with a packaging/protection role for oleosin.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful packaging/protection factor for stabilizing ectopic TAG in nonseed tissues; can be stacked with flux and assembly genes
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May not be the optimal lipid-droplet protein for nonseed tissues; LDAP proteins may outperform oleosin in future engineering
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors note oleosin may be suboptimal in nonseed oleaginous tissues and suggest LDAP as an alternative for future improvement.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy-dense storage oil; potential food, feed, oleochemical and biodiesel applications
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acylates diacylglycerol with acyl-CoA to form triacylglycerol in the final step of the Kennedy pathway
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a rate-limiting/final control step for TAG biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in transgenic potato tubers and leaves; relatively high in high-oil lines #68, #149 and #69 leaves; highest tuber expression associated with line #69
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse growth; mature tubers and postanthesis fully expanded leaves analyzed
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes strong TAG accumulation in potato tubers and leaves; associated with increased oleic acid and reduced polyunsaturated fatty acids in TAG
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transgenic overexpression; qRT-PCR; lipid TLC-GC profiling; fatty acid composition analysis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Constitutive expression of atDGAT1 in potato contributed to major TAG increases in tubers and leaves. 2. The highest tuber TAG line (#69) also showed the highest atDGAT1 expression, linking DGAT1 dosage to oil accumulation. 3. DGAT1 acts at the final Kennedy pathway step and likely helps shift acyl flux toward TAG and altered fatty acid composition.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key 'Pull' component for engineering oil-rich potato tubers; useful in gene stacking with precursor-supply and lipid-droplet packaging genes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Associated in the stacked lines with reduced starch, increased soluble sugars, altered membrane lipids and leaf oil accumulation due to constitutive expression
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper studies atDGAT1 only in a three-gene stack, so its individual quantitative contribution is not fully separable from WRI1 and OLEOSIN.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Energy storage oil; relevant to food, feed, and industrial oil production
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AP2/EREBP family transcription factor with two APETALA2 domains
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Fatty acid biosynthesis and precursor supply for TAG accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Genes encoding enzymes of late glycolysis and fatty acid biosynthesis, including BCCP subunit of ACCase, ACP, enoyl-ACP reductase, beta-ketoacyl-ACP reductase, plastidial pyruvate kinase, pyruvate dehydrogenase, and possibly FAD2/FAE1 as discussed
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates expression of genes supporting de novo fatty acid synthesis and precursor generation, thereby increasing flux into TAG biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive upstream activator ('Push' component) that boosts fatty acid production required for TAG accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in developing transgenic potato tubers at lower levels than DGAT1 and OLEOSIN; also detectable in leaves of high-lipid lines despite B33 promoter
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse growth; developing tubers and postanthesis leaves analyzed; promoter leakiness in leaves may relate to elevated sugars
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Sucrose-responsive/tuber developmental context via patatin promoter in transgene construct
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes TAG accumulation indirectly by increasing fatty acid biosynthetic capacity in potato tubers and leaves when stacked with DGAT1 and OLEOSIN
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transgenic overexpression in gene stack; qRT-PCR; lipid profiling; correlation with high TAG lines; literature-supported target-gene logic discussed by authors
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. atWRI1 was included as the 'Push' regulator to increase fatty acid biosynthetic flux in potato. 2. Its expression in transgenic tubers was moderate and generally lower than DGAT1/OLEOSIN, yet sufficient to support large TAG increases. 3. Leakiness of the B33 promoter enabled detectable leaf expression in high-oil lines, contributing to leaf TAG elevation.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful master regulator for boosting precursor supply in oil-engineering stacks, but likely best combined with downstream assembly and packaging genes
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In stacked lines, associated with carbon reallocation from starch to oil and soluble sugars; high expression may be selected against or developmentally constrained
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Causal role is inferred from the engineered stack and known WRI1 biology; this paper did not dissect direct potato target genes experimentally.
